g.text: UNSAFE PATH: raw memcpy (NEVER DO THIS)
Address Space: What access_ok() Actually Checks
Summary: The Four Guarantees of copy_from_user
Instruction: STAC (Set AC flag in RFLAGS)
Sets bit AC=1 in CPU RFLAGS register
CPU now permits ring-0 → ring-3 page access
This is a PRIVILEGED instruction (ring 0 only)
Copy loop: rep movsb / SSE / optimized
No kernel panic — graceful partial return
Instruction: CLAC (Clear AC flag in RFLAGS)
Sets bit AC=0 in CPU RFLAGS register
CPU once again blocks ring-0 → ring-3 access
Even if copy succeeded, SMAP is restored
Returns 0 → full success, all n bytes copied
Returns k → k bytes were NOT transferred
DO NOT confuse with bytes successfully copied
0x00 | int | insn // RIP-relative offset of faulting insn
0x04 | int | fixup // RIP-relative offset of fixup handler
0x08 | int | data // handler-specific data (e.g. reg index)
1. #PF fires at address X inside copy loop
NOT found? → kernel oops (bug in driver code)
Direction reversed: kernel buffer → user buffer
STAC/CLAC still required (SMAP blocks both dirs)
Same exception table mechanism for fault safety
Used in: read(), ioctl _IOR, seq_file output
0xFFFF800000000000 - 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF | KERNEL SPACE (128 TB) ← INVALID for user_ptr
access_ok: (user_ptr + n) <= TASK_SIZE_MAX — catches kernel-space forged ptrs
1. SECURITY: access_ok rejects forged kernel pointers
2. HARDWARE: STAC/CLAC works with SMAP silicon enforcement
3. SAFETY: exception table prevents panic on swapped pages
4. CONTRACT: returns residual bytes for partial-copy detection
All four are required simultaneously — no subset is sufficient

/* CORRECT: copy through the safe gate */
